entries.title: “Religions, which condemn the pleasures of sense, drive men to seek the pleasures of power. Throughout history power has been the vice of the ascetic.” — Bertrand Russell
entries.description: Religions, which condemn the pleasures of sense, drive men to seek the pleasures of power. Throughout history power has been the vice of the ascetic. — Bertrand Russell

entries.title: “In all of us, even in good men, there is a lawless wild-beast nature, which peers out in sleep” — Sokrates

entries.title: “War is not an adventure. It is a disease. It is like typhus.” — Antoine de Saint-Exupéry

entries.title: “I want to sing like the birds sing, not worrying about who hears or what they think.” — Rumi
entries.description: I want to sing like the birds sing, not worrying about who hears or what they think. — Rumi
entries.author: Rumi

entries.title: “The man who spends his time choosing one resort after another in a hunt for peace and quiet, will in every place he visits find something to prevent him from relaxing. The story is told that someone complained to Socrates that travelling abroad had never done him any good and received the reply: "What else can you expect, seeing that you always take yourself along with you when you go abroad?‟” — Seneca
entries.description: The man who spends his time choosing one resort after another in a hunt for peace and quiet, will in every place he visits find something to prevent him from relaxing.
